_meta.note: QC: lat ~250w arts-domain declarative low-contraction; lc ~85w peer Man/Woman; la ~80w varied openers; lcr wh-heavy with valid distractors.

lectures.subject: art_history
lectures.transcript: Impressionism was a movement in painting that began in France in the 1870s, and it changed the way artists thought about light. Impressionism is characterized by an attempt to capture the fleeting effects of light and atmosphere rather than precise detail. Earlier painters had worked mainly in studios, finishing their canvases slowly and aiming for smooth, almost invisible brushwork. The Impressionists did something different. They carried their materials outdoors and painted quickly, on the spot, trying to record a single moment before the light changed. To do this, they used short, broken strokes of pure color placed side by side. Up close, the surface can look like a chaos of dabs. But step back, and the eye blends the colors into shimmering water or sunlight on a field. The subjects changed too. Instead of grand historical scenes, the painters chose ordinary modern life: train stations, cafes, gardens, boating parties. At first, critics were hostile, complaining that the works looked unfinished, more like sketches than serious art. The very name began as an insult, taken from a Monet canvas titled Impression, Sunrise. Over time, though, the public came to value exactly what the critics had mocked. By focusing on perception itself, on how a scene strikes the eye in a single instant, the Impressionists opened the door to much of modern art.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How and why Impressionist painting broke with earlier traditions
options.B: The life of the painter Claude Monet
options.C: Techniques for mixing paint in a studio
options.D: The history of French train stations

questions.explanation: The talk traces what made Impressionism new and how it was eventually accepted.

questions.stem: How did the Impressionists create a sense of shimmering light?

options.A: By using short, broken strokes of pure color side by side
options.B: By blending colors smoothly and invisibly
options.C: By painting only indoors at night
options.D: By avoiding the color of sunlight

questions.explanation: "They used short, broken strokes of pure color placed side by side."

questions.stem: Why does the speaker mention the painting Impression, Sunrise?

options.A: To explain where the movement's name came from
options.B: To name Monet's most valuable work
options.C: To describe a train station scene
options.D: To prove the critics were correct

questions.explanation: The name 'Impressionism' came from that painting's title, originally used as an insult.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about the critics' early reaction?

options.A: It later proved to be the opposite of the public's lasting view
options.B: It was shared by Monet himself
options.C: It prevented the movement from spreading
options.D: It was based on the paintings' high cost

questions.explanation: "The public came to value exactly what the critics had mocked," implying the critics' view did not last.

lectures.subject: music
lectures.transcript: Jazz is a musical style that emerged in the United States, particularly in New Orleans, around the beginning of the twentieth century. What makes jazz distinctive is improvisation, the practice of inventing music spontaneously as it is performed. In most European classical music, performers play notes written in advance by a composer. In jazz, by contrast, a musician takes a basic melody and reshapes it on the spot, adding rhythms and notes that may never be played the same way twice. New Orleans was an unusually mixed city, where musical traditions from Africa, the Caribbean, and Europe met and blended. From the African tradition came complex, layered rhythms and a call-and-response pattern between players. From the European side came harmony and instruments such as the trumpet and clarinet. Early bands played at dances, parades, and even funerals, adapting their music to each occasion. As the style spread up the river to cities like Chicago, recordings carried it to a national audience, and listeners who had never visited New Orleans could suddenly hear the sound at home. Some critics dismissed jazz as noisy and undisciplined. Musicians, however, treated improvisation as a demanding skill that required deep knowledge of harmony and rhythm; a soloist had to create faster than the music moved. That balance between individual freedom and the structure of the group is what gives jazz its lasting energy.

questions.stem: What does the speaker mainly discuss?

options.A: What defines jazz and where it came from
options.C: The history of the city of Chicago
options.D: Why classical music uses written notes

questions.explanation: The lecture explains improvisation and the mixed origins of jazz.

questions.stem: According to the speaker, what came from the African musical tradition?

options.A: Layered rhythms and call-and-response
options.C: Written musical scores
options.D: The idea of the funeral parade

questions.explanation: "From the African tradition came complex, layered rhythms and a call-and-response pattern."

questions.stem: Why does the speaker describe New Orleans as an unusually mixed city?

options.A: To explain how different musical traditions blended into jazz
options.B: To argue that jazz could only be played there
options.C: To list the city's main exports

questions.explanation: The mix of African, Caribbean, and European traditions is presented as the source of jazz.

questions.stem: What can be inferred about the musicians' view of improvisation?

options.A: They considered it a serious, skilled discipline
options.B: They agreed it was noisy and undisciplined
options.C: They thought it required no training

questions.explanation: They "treated improvisation as a demanding skill that required deep knowledge," countering the critics.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: I've been listening to the same playlists for months. Can you recommend something new?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Sure. What mood are you after, something calm or more upbeat?

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Upbeat, definitely. Something I can play while I'm running.

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Then try that new electronic album everyone's posting about. The beats are perfect for a run.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Is it on the usual app?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Yeah, it's everywhere. I'll send you the link after class.

questions.stem: What are the speakers mainly discussing?

options.A: Music the woman could listen to while running
options.C: How to fix a broken playlist app

questions.explanation: The woman wants new upbeat music for running; the man recommends an album.

questions.stem: What will the man do after class?

options.A: Send the woman a link to the album
options.B: Go for a run with her
options.C: Buy her the album

questions.explanation: "I'll send you the link after class."

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: I'm making pasta for a few friends on Friday. Any tips?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Don't overcook the noodles. Take them out a minute early; they keep cooking in the sauce.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: Good to know. What about the sauce itself?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Keep it simple. A good tomato sauce with fresh basil beats anything complicated.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: That sounds manageable. Should I make a salad too?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Definitely, something light, so people don't fill up before the main course.

options.A: A pasta dinner for friends
options.C: A trip to a restaurant

questions.explanation: "I'm making pasta for a few friends on Friday."

questions.stem: What does the man advise about the noodles?

options.A: Take them out a minute early
options.C: Rinse them in cold water

questions.explanation: "Take them out a minute early; they keep cooking in the sauce."

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: How's the new job at the bookstore going?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Pretty well. The hours fit around my classes, which is the main thing.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: That's lucky. Is the work itself interesting?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Mostly shelving and helping customers find things. It's quiet, but I see all the new titles.

conversation.speaker: Woman
conversation.text: And a staff discount, I bet?

conversation.speaker: Man
conversation.text: Twenty percent. I'm spending half my paycheck on books already.

questions.stem: Why does the man like his new job?

options.A: The hours fit around his classes
options.B: It pays better than his old job
options.C: He can study while working
options.D: It is close to his dorm

questions.explanation: "The hours fit around my classes, which is the main thing."

questions.stem: What does the woman correctly guess about the job?

options.A: That it comes with a staff discount
options.C: That the man dislikes it

questions.explanation: She says "a staff discount, I bet?" and the man confirms twenty percent.

announcements.announcement: Good morning, everyone. Before we begin, I want to remind you that the deadline for your research proposals is this Friday at 5 p.m. Please submit them through the course website, not by email. I've also posted three sample proposals from previous years so you can see the level of detail expected. If you're struggling to narrow your topic, my office hours are Wednesday afternoon, and I'm happy to talk it through. A clear, focused question is worth more than a broad one.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of the announcement?

options.A: To remind students about the proposal deadline and offer help
options.C: To introduce a guest speaker
options.D: To explain a change in the grading scale

questions.explanation: The professor states the deadline, how to submit, and offers office hours.

questions.stem: How should students submit their proposals?

options.A: Through the course website
options.C: On paper in class

questions.explanation: "Please submit them through the course website, not by email."

announcements.announcement: Attention, residents. Due to scheduled electrical work, the power in the east wing of the dormitory will be shut off this Saturday from 8 a.m. to noon. Elevators and hallway lighting will not be available during that time, so please use the stairs and plan accordingly. The west wing will not be affected. If you rely on medical equipment that needs power, contact the residence office today so we can make arrangements.

questions.stem: What will happen on Saturday morning?

options.A: The power in the east wing will be shut off
options.C: The elevators will be repaired in the west wing

questions.explanation: "The power in the east wing of the dormitory will be shut off this Saturday from 8 a.m. to noon."

questions.stem: What should residents who use medical equipment do?

options.A: Contact the residence office today
options.B: Use the elevators instead of the stairs
options.C: Move to the east wing

questions.explanation: "Contact the residence office today so we can make arrangements."

shortResponse.speaker: Where can I pick up my student ID card?

options.A: At the registrar's office on the first floor.
options.C: You'll need a photo for it.

shortResponse.explanation: Answers the 'where' directly; B/C/D answer when/requirement/hours instead.

shortResponse.speaker: Why is the reading room closed today?

options.A: They're installing new carpet.
options.C: I have a book to return.

shortResponse.explanation: Gives a reason ('why'); the others answer when/personal/where.

shortResponse.speaker: How do I sign up for the campus shuttle?

options.A: There's a form on the transportation website.
options.C: The stop is right by the gym.

shortResponse.explanation: Explains the method ('how'); the others give frequency/location/personal habit.

shortResponse.speaker: Did you finish the lab report?

options.A: Almost, I just need to add the graphs.
options.B: The lab is on the third floor.
options.C: Reports are usually due on Friday.

shortResponse.explanation: A direct yes/no-style answer about progress; the others are off-topic.

shortResponse.speaker: What time does the career fair start?

options.A: I think it opens at ten.
options.C: Bring a few copies of your resume.
options.D: A lot of companies are coming.

shortResponse.explanation: Answers 'what time'; the others answer where/advice/who.

shortResponse.speaker: I can't find a quiet place to study tonight.

options.A: Have you tried the third floor of the library?
options.C: The library has a lot of books.

shortResponse.explanation: Offers a helpful suggestion to the implied problem; the others don't address it.
